turns.content: I'm stuck on this exercise about running a periodic task. I thought if I just use tokio::time::sleep(Duration::from_secs(1)) inside my loop, it will wake up the executor and run again right away without waiting for anything else.

That's a common misunderstanding. Using tokio::time::sleep alone won't automatically restart your task in the same way an imperative loop does because async code runs concurrently, not sequentially like regular loops. The executor might pause waiting for other tasks to finish or use up slots on the runtime threads.
Oh, so I need something else? Like maybe tokio::time::timeout with some interval logic?

You're getting closer but mixing up concepts. tokio::time::timeout is for wrapping a future that might take too long, not for scheduling periodic tasks. For intervals you should use Instant and Duration to track elapsed time between executions.
Right, so I keep an instant timestamp of when the last run happened and compare it against now()? But how do I actually schedule the next one?

You update your Instant after each successful run, then use tokio::time::sleep with the difference between now() and that stored instant to create a natural pause. That way the sleep duration adjusts if tasks finish faster or slower.
Got it. So instead of hardcoding sleep values, I calculate how long has passed since my last execution using Instant and then sleep for whatever's left until the next interval?
Exactly. That dynamic approach handles race conditions better than fixed timeouts. Just make sure you're storing that Instant outside your loop scope so it persists across async iterations.

I'm following this talk on async patterns and I'm stuck. Why does tokio::time::sleep just take a Duration argument instead of being like std::thread::sleep which takes milliseconds? It feels weird that my code isn't explicitly saying how long to wait in seconds.

That's actually the core design choice. Since everything in Rust is driven by integers, you don't need floating point numbers for time. The Duration type wraps that integer count of nanoseconds so your code stays consistent with other numeric types like usize or u64.
Got it on the storage side, but what about when I need to actually stop a running task? Like if an API call times out after 5 seconds?

That's where tokio::time::timeout comes in. You wrap your async block inside timeout(Instant::now() + Duration::from_secs(5), your_function). It returns an Option<Result>, so you can check if the task finished before the deadline or if it was cancelled.
Okay, so I can chain timeouts together? What if I need to run a periodic health check every 30 seconds instead of just a one-off?

You'd combine tokio::time::sleep with the timeout pattern. You create an interval using Instant and Duration, then repeatedly call your sleep function to wait for that duration before running your health check logic.

Wait, I think I'm starting to see how this fits into my existing error handling. If I use tokio::time::timeout and the task fails, does it automatically propagate errors or do I need to manually unwrap them?

The timeout function returns an Option<Result>, so you get a Result if successful or Err(TimeoutError) if cancelled. You'll typically match on that to decide whether to log the timeout and continue, or return the error up your stack.
